coredata.dc:title: Effects of ultrasound pulse parameters on cavitation properties of flowing microbubbles under physiologically relevant conditions
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Mouwen
Li, Fan
Han, Tao
Yu, Alfred C.H.
Qin, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Acoustic cavitation from ultrasound-driven microbubbles can induce diverse bioeffects that are useful in clinical therapy. However, lack of control over the cavitation activity of flowing microbubbles results in unwanted treatment regions in the targeted tissue, which influences the therapeutic efficacy and bio-safety. The aim of this study is to understand the relationship between the ultrasound pulse parameters and cavitation properties of flowing microbubbles, including the type (and transition between types), threshold, intensity and temporal distribution of cavitation. An in vitro physiological-flow phantom was fabricated, in which the microbubbles had a constant velocity, and were sonicated to a 1-MHz focused transducer at a wide range of peak negative pressures (PNPs) (0.10–1.28 MPa), pulse repetition frequencies (PRFs) (1–200 Hz) and pulse lengths (PLs) (10–400 μs). The signals from the flowing bubbles were passively detected by another 7.5-MHz plane transducer. From detailed time- and frequency-domain analysis, we found 1). The occurrence of stable cavitation (SC) and inertial cavitation (IC) depended on PNP and PL when the PRF was below a critical value (PRF threshold) that related to the fluid velocity and PNP full width at half maximum diameter of the transducer. 2) Below the PRF threshold, the PL had no influence on the temporal distribution of SC intensity; however, above the PRF threshold, the SC properties depended on the PL because of acoustically-driven diffusion. Specifically, at shorter PLs, the SC intensity had a uniform temporal distribution and was independent of the PRF; at longer PLs, the SC intensity correlated negatively with the PRF. 3) Below the PRF threshold, the IC properties were independent of the PRF. Increasing the PRF above the PRF threshold caused the IC intensity to decrease with a non-uniform temporal distribution. These results indicate that the fluid velocity and a pulsed acoustic field influence the number and properties of the replenished bubbles into the targeted region, resulting in the change of cavitation properties. In future therapeutic applications, the physiological fluid conditions must be taken into consideration to design reasonable pulse parameters and achieve desirable cavitation properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Flowing microbubbles
Stable cavitation
Inertial cavitation
Passive cavitation detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microbubbles and fabrication of the agarose-gel tube phantom
Experimental platform to mimic in vitro physiological flow
Passive cavitation detection and signal sampling
Categorization of cavitation evolution of the flowing microbubbles
Dependence of cavitation evolution of flowing microbubbles on the PNP
Influence of PL on the cavitation thresholds and intensity of flowing microbubbles
Influence of PRF on the intensity and temporal distribution of stable cavitation for short PLs
Influence of PRF on the intensity and temporal distribution of stable cavitation for long PLs
Influence of the PRF on the intensity and temporal distribution of inertial cavitation
Cavitation evolution under an appropriate PRF and fluid condition
Effect of PRF on the PL dependence of stable cavitation properties
Effect of PRF on inertial cavitation properties

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. Schematic representation of the in vitro physiological fluid system for the microbubbles population with constant velocity. A spherical, focused transducer and a plane transducer were used to sonicate and passively detect cavitation signals, respectively. B. The yellow region marked by the dashed circle is the focal zone of the focused transducer. The left and right sides of the focal zone are the inflow and outflow regions of the tube, respectively. The FWHW lateral distance of the transducer was ∼3.0 mm. C. 2D profile of the PNP in the focal zone (focal distance: 65 mm) of the transducer (corresponding to the center of the tube in the focal region). The spatially averaged PNP was about 0.68 MPa within the FWHW region.

Typical power spectra plots of the different cavitation behaviors: a. no oscillation (red line) (control solution without microbubbles), b. small-amplitude oscillation (light grey line), c. stable cavitation (large-amplitude oscillation) (dark grey line), d. the mixed stable and inertial cavitation (light black line) and e. intense inertial cavitation (black line). These cavitation signals obtained at 0.10 MPa, 0.23 MPa, 0.42 MPa and 0.68 MPa (black line) with 100-μs PL, 5-Hz PRF, and a fluid velocity of 16 mm/s.

A, B, C Effect of PNP (in the range of 0.10–1.28 MPa) on the transition of flowing microbubbles from stable to inertial cavitation at PLs of 10–400 μs and a PRF of 5 Hz. 150 pulses were used to calculate U/B
' , B and U. D. Temporal distributions of U/B
' (black line), B
' (red line) and U (Blue line) at representative PNPs of 0.10–1.28 MPa, a PL of 100 μs and a PRF of 5 Hz.

A,B. Effect of PL (in the range of 10–800 μs) on the intensity of stable cavitation at PNPs of 0.10–0.23 MPa (A), and on the intensity of inertial cavitation at PNPs of 0.42–1.28 MPa (B). In both cases, the PRF was 5 Hz. 150 pulses were used to calculate U/B
' and B.

Effect of PRF (in the range of 1–200 Hz) on the intensity (A) and temporal distribution of stable cavitation of flowing microbubbles at PLs of 50 μs (B1) and 200 μs (B2). The PNP was 0.23 MPa and the fluid velocity was 16 or 24 mm/s. C. Corresponding spectra of cavitation for 150 acoustic pulses at a fluid velocity of 16 mm/s and a PL of 50 μs (C1) and 200 μs (C2).

Effect of PRF (in the range of 1–200 Hz) on the intensity (A) and temporal distribution of inertial cavitation of flowing microbubbles at PLs of 400 μs (B1) and 50 μs (B2). The PNP was 0.42 MPa and the fluid velocity was 16 or 24 mm/s. C. Corresponding spectra of cavitation activity for 150 acoustic pulses at a fluid velocity of 16 mm/s and a PL of 400 μs (C1) and 50 μs (C2).

head.ce:title: Effects of ultrasound pulse parameters on cavitation properties of flowing microbubbles under physiologically relevant conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Instrument Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Instrument Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Ultrasound, Shanghai General Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acoustic cavitation from ultrasound-driven microbubbles can induce diverse bioeffects that are useful in clinical therapy. However, lack of control over the cavitation activity of flowing microbubbles results in unwanted treatment regions in the targeted tissue, which influences the therapeutic efficacy and bio-safety. The aim of this study is to understand the relationship between the ultrasound pulse parameters and cavitation properties of flowing microbubbles, including the type (and transition between types), threshold, intensity and temporal distribution of cavitation. An in vitro physiological-flow phantom was fabricated, in which the microbubbles had a constant velocity, and were sonicated to a 1-MHz focused transducer at a wide range of peak negative pressures (PNPs) (0.10–1.28 MPa), pulse repetition frequencies (PRFs) (1–200 Hz) and pulse lengths (PLs) (10–400 μs). The signals from the flowing bubbles were passively detected by another 7.5-MHz plane transducer. From detailed time- and frequency-domain analysis, we found 1). The occurrence of stable cavitation (SC) and inertial cavitation (IC) depended on PNP and PL when the PRF was below a critical value (PRF threshold) that related to the fluid velocity and PNP full width at half maximum diameter of the transducer. 2) Below the PRF threshold, the PL had no influence on the temporal distribution of SC intensity; however, above the PRF threshold, the SC properties depended on the PL because of acoustically-driven diffusion. Specifically, at shorter PLs, the SC intensity had a uniform temporal distribution and was independent of the PRF; at longer PLs, the SC intensity correlated negatively with the PRF. 3) Below the PRF threshold, the IC properties were independent of the PRF. Increasing the PRF above the PRF threshold caused the IC intensity to decrease with a non-uniform temporal distribution. These results indicate that the fluid velocity and a pulsed acoustic field influence the number and properties of the replenished bubbles into the targeted region, resulting in the change of cavitation properties. In future therapeutic applications, the physiological fluid conditions must be taken into consideration to design reasonable pulse parameters and achieve desirable cavitation properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Microbubbles, which are typically composed of a gas nuclei and a lipid shell, can be used to nonlinearly scatter ultrasound, improving ultrasonic imaging for clinical diagnosis [1,2]. These microbubbles are driven into volumetric oscillations upon exposure to the alternating positive and negative acoustic pressure cycles of the ultrasound wave [3]. This acoustically driven behavior, known as acoustic cavitation, can be categorized as stable cavitation (SC), inertial cavitation (IC) or a mixture of both according to the nature of the radial oscillations [4,5]. SC, in which microbubbles radically oscillate in a periodic manner, is characterized by sub-harmonic and ultra-harmonic emissions [6,7]. SC has been shown to stimulate endocytosis and enhance membrane permeability for the delivery of therapeutic agents into cells [8,9], and to facilitate penetration of thrombolytic agents into clots [10,11]. IC, in which an unstable expansion phase results in the violent collapse of microbubble, is characterized by broadband acoustic emissions [7,12]. IC has been found to perforate the plasma membrane and open the blood–brain barrier for macromolecular delivery [13–15]. However, the inappropriate type and/or intensity may result in regions of the targeted tissue being under- or over-treated, thus affecting the therapeutic efficacy and safety [16–18]. Therefore, to achieve effective delivery and minimize deleterious effects, it is crucial to gain a deeper understanding of the factors relating to cavitation properties of flowing microbubbles.

The acoustic and non-acoustic factors, such as ultrasound pulse parameters (i.e. pulse repetition frequency (PRF), center frequency, pulse length (PL) and peak negative pressure (PNP)), microbubble properties (e.g. shell compositions, bubble size and gas type) correlate with the type and intensity of cavitation [19]. The PNP related to the amplitude of radial oscillation dictates the type of cavitation and thus the concomitant bioeffects [5,20]. The center frequency affects the absorption time, in which microbubbles absorb acoustic energy, and correlates negatively with the amplitude and duration of radial oscillation [21,22]. Increasing the PL could raise the energy absorption of the cavitating bubbles, thereby reducing the thresholds of SC and IC [23–25]. However, a long PL could also cause instability of the microbubble shell, resulting in acoustically driven gas diffusion and bubble rupture [26–28]. On the other hand, with respect to the cavitation properties caused by the sonication of aqueous solutions, previous studies indicated the decrease of inverse pulse duty ratio (=pulse period/PL) could reduce cavitation thresholds and the subharmonics intensity [29–31]. Moreover, it is necessary to use higher ultrasound intensities to generate cavitation in pulsed acoustic filed than in continuous field [29].

However, most of the results described above were obtained in static systems. A flowing environment with variable fluid velocity and boundary conditions complicates the spatiotemporal distribution of cavitation activity in the focal volume, possibly producing over- and under-treated therapeutic areas. Indeed, previous studies proposed models for characterizing destruction–replenishment kinetics of microbubbles for diagnostic imaging [32]; however, this has not yet been conducted for therapeutic applications. Conditions of a long PL and high pressure with a PRF on the order of Hz are widely used to deliver drugs across the blood–brain barrier [33,34]. However, these ultrasound conditions might cause non-uniform spatial-distribution of cavitation activity (inflow bias) in the focal region [35,36]. A lower PNP, longer pulse intervals and shorter PLs were found to improve the spatial distribution of SC activity by persisting SC in the targeted region, thereby improving the uniformity and magnitude of drug delivery [16,37]. Moreover, several studies showed that a sufficient time interval between pulses is required to allow microbubbles to reperfuse in the focal region to produce specific bioeffects [11,38,39]. Previous studies also determined that the PRF, which influences the number of the replenished flowing bubbles in the focal volume before arrival of an acoustic pulse, has a major role in determining the predictability and spatial uniformity of the cavitation dynamics, particularly for IC activity [40]. Thus, it is crucial to select a proper PRF with respect to the physiological fluid velocity [40]. However, other studies showed that short pulses could instigate a uniform distribution of IC activity even at higher PRF (i.e. not restricted by the fluid velocity) [41]. These studies elucidated the effect of the ultrasound conditions on cavitation properties from different points, and presented some pulse sequences for applications, however, it is still lack of mechanistic understanding how a wide range of acoustic pulse parameters affects the cavitation properties of flowing bubbles.

In this study, we aim to comprehensively understand the relationship between the ultrasound pulse parameters and the cavitation properties of bubbles under physiological fluid conditions. We sonicated flowing microbubbles in an in vitro phantom using a focused transducer under a wide range of acoustic parameters. We used passive cavitation detection to receive the acoustic signals from the flowing microbubbles. Using detailed analysis of the time- and frequency-domain characteristics, we clarified how the pulse field and fluid conditions influence the type, threshold, intensity and the temporal distribution of microbubbles cavitation. This study will help optimize the pulse parameters with respect to fluid conditions for producing adjustable and controllable cavitation activity for applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Microbubbles and fabrication of the agarose-gel tube phantom
ce:section.ce:para: An agarose-gel tube phantom was fabricated to simulate the vasculature, in which bubbles flowed at a constant rate. Specifically, the degassed agarose-gel solution, made as previously described, was poured into a self-made cubic mold (60 mm × 42 mm × 40 mm), in the center of which a glass rod with 2-mm diameter was inserted. After 30 min, the solution was naturally cooled and condensed to form a tissue-mimicking phantom, from which the glass rod was carefully extracted, leaving a passage for the flowing bubbles. The measured acoustic properties of this agarose-gel phantom were similar to those of soft tissue of the human body [42], making this a suitable phantom for the vasculature.

To maintain good stability, SonoVue microbubbles (Bracco Research, Switzerland) was freshly reconstituted before every experiment by mixing the lysophilisate with 5 mL physiological saline solution, forming a suspension containing approximately 2–5 × 108 bubbles per milliliter. Prior to acoustic exposure, to ensure clinical relevance, the initial bubble solution was diluted to 5 vol% with physiological saline solution, reaching an estimated concentration of 1–2.5 × 107 microbubbles per milliliter. This bubbles concentration was approximately 5–10× the acceptable dose for clinical application which is about 20 µL/Kg, corresponding to 2 × 106 bubbles per milliliter, which was calculated for an average male mass of 70 kg with a blood volume of 5 L [43] (Similar concentration was also used in the previous studies [16]). This concentration was also safety for therapeutic application because previous studies in primates showed the bubbles dose up to 10 mL/kg did not adversely affect the blood pressure or other hemodynamics properties [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental platform to mimic in vitro physiological flow
Flowing microbubble system
ce:section.ce:para: A customized in vitro experimental platform, as illustrated in Fig. 1 , was developed to apply ultrasound to microbubbles flowing at velocities typical of physiological fluids. The agarose-gel tube phantom was half-submerged and horizontally positioned in a tank containing degassed and deionized water. The microbubble solution was injected into the agarose-gel tube with a syringe pump (AJ5815, Angel electronic equipment Co., Ltd, China), which could provide a known and controllable fluid velocity from 0 to 24 mm/s (corresponding to a 2-mm-diameter tube). The absorber (UA-1, Institute of Acoustics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China) was placed at the bottom of the tank to avoid disturbance to the acoustic field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasound emission and calibration
ce:section.ce:para: A 1-MHz single-element, spherical-segment focused transducer (active diameter of 65 mm and focal length of 65 mm) (Advanced Devices, Wakefield, MA), coupled with a cone-shaped catheter filled with degassed water, was mounted on a customized device. This ensured that the incident surface of the transducer was kept at a distance of 65 mm from the exposure region in the agarose-tube phantom. The transducer was driven by a sinusoidal signal with a 1-MHz center frequency and different PRFs, PLs and voltage amplitudes, which was generated by a signal generator (33120A, Agilent Technologies, USA) and amplified by a 50-dB radio frequency power amplifier (E&I 2100L; Electronics &Innovation Ltd, USA). Before the experiments, the two-dimension profile of the PNP in the focal volume was mapped by automatically moving a capsule hydrophone (HGL-0200, ONDA, USA) with a three-axis translation stage (ASTS-01, ONDA, USA) and is shown in Fig. 1C. The focused beam had a PNP full width at half-maximum (FWHM) diameter of approximately 3.0 mm in the lateral direction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Passive cavitation detection and signal sampling
ce:section.ce:para: Another plane transducer (7.5-MHz; 13-mm active diameter) (Advanced Devices, Wakefield, MA) was positioned through the center of the focused transducer such that the two transducers were coaxially aligned. The acoustic signals from the exposed flowing bubbles were passively received and converted to electrical signals by this transducer, and then were amplified by a pulser-receiver (5077PR, Olympus, USA) with variable gain. The amplified signals were subsequently acquired at 50 million samples per second by a 14-bit digitizer (Gage, USA), and transmitted to a host computer for processing. The digitizer was triggered by the rising edge of a square wave signal, which was synchronized with the output pulse of the signal generator, as shown in Fig. 1A and B. To prevent loss of the acoustic signals, the sampling period was set to exceed the pulse length. All sampling parameters were set in LabVIEW (National Instruments, USA) controlling software in the host computer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signal processing and analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
To characterize the type and intensity of acoustic cavitation in the focal volume, the recorded temporal signal Xi (n) (n = 0, 1, ···, N − 1; i = 1, 2, ···, M) (N: the number of sampling points in a sampling period T; M: the number of the sampling period) in a sampling period was first processed by fast Fourier transform to obtain the frequency spectrum:
(1)
X i
k
=
∑
n = 0
N - 1

n
e
- j
2 π
N
k n
( k =
0 , 1
, ⋯ , N - 1 )

k was the frequency point in frequency spectrum. To characterize the intensity of the signal at different frequencies, the power spectrum (p) of the signal was calculated by
(2)
P i
k
=
X i
( k )
2

A comb band-pass filter, with a fixed 0.1-MHz bandwidth that was centered at odd multiples of half the driving frequency (1 MHz), was first applied to separate the ultra-harmonics from a frequency range encompassing potential harmonics and broadband signals, and then the intensity of ultra-harmonics (
U i
) in a sampling period T was calculated by
(3)
U i
= T ×
∑
a = 1
8
∑
c = 1
131
P i
c
where a and c are the number of ultra-harmonics and the number of frequency points in the 0.1-MHz bandwidth, respectively. Thus, the intensity of SC (U) during the whole exposure time can be obtained by
(4)
U =
∑
i = 1
M

is

∑

Uis and Uic were the ultra-harmonics intensity from the flowing microbubbles and the control solution without microbubbles, respectively. Another notch filter with 0.2-MHz stopband, which was centered at the integer multiplies of the driving frequency and the odd multiples of half the driving frequency, was applied to trap the broadband components. The intensity of broadband emissions (
B i
) in a sampling period was calculated by
(5)

= T ×

where d and e are the number of broadband components and the number of frequency points in every broadband, respectively. Thus, the whole intensity of IC (B) in the whole exposure time can be obtained by
(6)

Bis and Bic were the broadband intensity from the flowing microbubbles population and the control solution, respectively.

In addition, ultra-harmonics intensity could be also enhanced by the rise of broadband emissions. To evaluate that the enhancement of ultra-harmonics originated from large-amplitude oscillation or violent collapse, another notch filter with 0.45-MHz stopband, which was centered at the frequencies same with those in the above notch filter, was applied to trap some of the broadband components, then the partial intensity of broadband emissions (
B
i

) in a sampling period was calculated by
(7)

' = T ×

Thus, the partial intensity of IC (B

(8)

B
i s
'
and
B

'
were the partial broadband intensity from the flowing microbubbles and the control solution, respectively. Noted that the number of the frequency points (65 × 16 × M) for
B '
was approximately equal to that (131 × 8 × M) for U. The corresponding ratio of ultra-harmonics to partial broadband intensity (U/B
' ) was used to evaluate the source of the enhanced ultra-harmonics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Categorization of cavitation evolution of the flowing microbubbles
ce:section.ce:para:
To characterize the cavitation evolution of flowing bubbles driven by pulsed ultrasound, the following parameters were defined to distinguish stable oscillation, violent collapse and the mixture of these two behaviors in the used experimental setup. Fig. 2 depicts the typical power spectra plots of the different cavitation behaviors of flowing bubbles exposed to 150 acoustic pulses with 100-μs PL, 5-Hz PRF, 16 mm/s fluid velocity and different PNPs. Only fundamental frequency were observed in the control solution (Fig. 2a), indicative of no cavitation activity occurrence. Besides fundamental frequency, harmonic signals were present in the frequency spectrum (Fig. 2b), determining that bubbles underwent small-amplitude oscillations. The corresponding U/B
' and B were less than 1.2 and 1.5 × 10−7 V2 S, respectively. Pronounced ultra-harmonics in Fig. 2c indicated that the bubbles oscillated with large amplitude and underwent SC. The corresponding ratio of U/B
' was greater than 1.2 and B was less than 1.5 × 10−7 V2 S, Fig. 2d showed both the broadband and ultra-harmonic components were enhanced, indicative of the occurrence of the mixed SC and IC in bubbles population. The corresponding U/B
' and B were more than 1.2 and 1.5 × 10−7 V2 S, respectively, By contrast, in Fig. 2e, broadband emissions and ultra-harmonics were further enhanced and minimized, respectively, showing that the flowing bubbles violently collapsed and underwent intense IC, resulting in B > 1.5 × 10−7 V2 S and U/B
' < 1.2. Thus, the different cavitation behaviors could be differentiated according to the values of U/B
' and B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistics analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In both the control solution without microbubbles and the solution with flowing microbubbles, a minimum of three replicates experiments were performed under all ultrasound pulse shapes investigated, and then the mean and standard deviation were calculated for each set of conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results
Dependence of cavitation evolution of flowing microbubbles on the PNP
ce:section.ce:para:
We first investigated the effect of PNP on the transition of flowing microbubbles from stable to inertial cavitation. 150 pulses with PNPs of 0.10–1.28 MPa and PLs of 10–400 μs were emitted at a 5-Hz PRF, which ensured the focal region was completely replenished by flowing microbubbles in the off-time between two successive pulses when the fluid velocity was set at 16 mm/s. Fig. 3 show the relationship between the PNP at different PLs and U/B
' , B and U. When the PNP was below 0.1 MPa, U/B
' was less than 1.2, B and U approached to the control level for PLs of 10–400 μs, respectively. Under these conditions, almost no ultra-harmonics and broadband emissions were generated from the flowing microbubbles. When the PNP was increased to 0.23 MPa, U/B
' notably increased from 1.4 to 4.2 with the increasing U as the PL was increased from 20 to 400 μs, whereas the broadband emissions stabilized at the level from the control solution (also see power spectra in Fig. 2a and c). Therefore, 0.23 MPa PNP, at which the emissions mainly included ultra-harmonics and no broadband components, can be considered as the lowest PNP (threshold) for triggering large-amplitude oscillations (SC). Broadband emissions started to notably increase with ultra-harmonic components when further increasing the PNP to 0.42 MPa at PLs above 20 μs. The corresponding B was more than 1.5 × 10−7 V2 S (reaching ∼4 × 10−7 V2 S at 0.68 MPa), U increased to ∼0.7 × 10−7 V2 S at 0.68 MPa, and U/B
' was above 1.2 (see power spectra in Fig. 2d), Therefore, 0.42 MPa could be considered as the threshold for triggering IC. For the PNP above 0.42 MPa and below 0.86 MPa, ultra-harmonic components always appeared with the broadband emissions (due to U/B
' > 1.2), suggesting that the flowing microbubbles population exhibits mixed behavior of large-amplitude oscillation and rapid collapse under these conditions. Thus, Mixed SC and IC occurred from a PNP of 0.42–0.86 MPa. Above 0.86 MPa, Although B and U steadily increased for the PL range of 10–400 μs, U/B
' gradually decreased to less than 1.2. Thus the increased U should originate from the rising B, not from the large-amplitude oscillation, indicative of totally collapsing of flowing bubbles population (also see power spectra in Fig. 2e).

Representative temporal distributions of U/B
' , B
' and U at different PNPs and 100-μs PL are shown in Fig. 3D1–6. All of U/B
' , B
' and U uniformly distributed during the entirely effective sonication time (150 × 100 μs) at different PNPs. The amplitude of U/B
' reached a peak (about 3.2) at 0.23-MPa PNP, and decreased to a minimum value (about 1.0) at 1.06 MPa. Almost no broadband emissions were observed at PNPs below 0.23 MPa. Above 0.42 MPa, the broadband emissions gradually increased with increasing PNP. These results show that the cavitation evolution of flowing microbubbles depends on the PNP under a fixed PL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of PL on the cavitation thresholds and intensity of flowing microbubbles
ce:section.ce:para:
Reorganizing the data from Fig. 3, we further analyzed the effect of PL on the cavitation thresholds and intensity of flowing bubbles. Fig. 4 A and B show the obtained U/B
' and B for PLs of 10–800 μs at different PNPs. At 0.10-MPa PNP, below the pressure threshold for initiating SC, U/B
'
was less than 1.2 for PLs below 400 μs. By contrast, U/B
' exceeded 1.2 for PLs above 400 μs, and reached 2.76 for a PL of 800 μs, suggesting that long pulses can trigger the large-amplitude oscillation of flowing bubbles even at the lower PNPs. A similar trend was also observed at 0.23-MPa PNP (i.e. at the SC threshold). At PLs above 20 μs, there were strong ultra-harmonic emissions; however, at a PL of 10 μs, the U/B
'
was below 1.2, indicating that there were no ultra-harmonics. This result indicates that increasing pulse duration may reduce the SC threshold of the flowing bubbles, and increase the intensity of SC in the focal region.

According to the results in the section above, the IC threshold for a 50-μs PL is 0.42-MPa PNP, which corresponds to B ≈ 2 × 10−7 V2 S. However, at shorter PLs, a PNP of 0.42 MPa (e.g. 10 μs) does not cause substantial broadband energy (B < 1.5 × 10−7 V2 S), suggesting that 0.42 MPa cannot be taken as the IC threshold at PLs below 50 μs. Moreover, at PLs above 400 μs, a PNP of 0.36 MPa generates broadband emissions with B approaching 1.5 × 10−7 V2 S (data not shown). These results indicate that the IC threshold of flowing bubbles reduces with the increasing PL. Moreover, Fig. 4B also shows that the broadband emissions intensity gradually rose with increasing PL, reaching a maximum value above 400-μs PL, which indicates that the IC threshold weakly depends on the PL, and that the IC intensity correlates positively with the PL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of PRF on the intensity and temporal distribution of stable cavitation for short PLs
ce:section.ce:para:
We then determined the effect of PRF on the SC intensity and temporal distribution of the flowing microbubbles. We used two different fluid velocities (16 or 24 mm/s) and a PNP of 0.23 MPa, which triggers ultra-harmonic components without notable broadband emissions for PLs above 20 μs. The intensity and temporal distribution of SC were evaluated for PRFs of 1–200 Hz for 150 short (50 μs) or long (200 μs) pulses, as shown in Fig. 5
A and B.

For the short pulses (50 μs), there was no notable dependence of the ultra-harmonics intensity on the PRF in the range of 1–200 Hz, as can be seen in Fig. 5A. In fact, only increasing the fluid velocity produced a higher ultra-harmonics emission. These observations suggest that the SC intensity of the flowing microbubbles in the focal region is independent of the PRF for short PLs. As can be seen in Fig. 5B1, the SC intensity in the focal volume was relatively constant over 150 pulses, and had approximately the same temporal amplitude at PRFs of 5, 10 and 200 Hz (representative values taken within the PRF range studied of 1–200 Hz), suggesting that the temporal distribution of SC intensity was independent of the PRF. In addition, it can be observed in Fig. 5C1 that the ultra-harmonics were present throughout 150 pulses at PRFs of 5, 10 and 200 Hz, thus leading to a uniform distribution of SC intensity in the temporal domain. These results suggest that at short PLs, the PRF has no influence on the number and properties of the flowing microbubbles in the focal region, resulting in almost same ultra-harmonics emission throughout the exposure period.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of PRF on the intensity and temporal distribution of stable cavitation for long PLs
ce:section.ce:para: When a long PL (200 μs) was used with the same PNP and PRF range as above for the short PL (0.23 MPa and 1–200 Hz, respectively), different intensity and temporal distribution of SC were observed, as shown in Fig. 5A and B2. Specifically, no significant change was observed in the ultra-harmonics intensity as the PRF was increased from 1 to 5 Hz at fixed fluid velocity. Moreover, at PRF below 5 Hz, the ultra-harmonics intensity in 24 mm/s velocity was almost two times larger than that in 16 mm/s velocity, indicating the ultra-harmonics intensity markedly increased with the increasing fluid velocity. However, the ultra-harmonics intensity at both fluid velocities rapidly dropped when the PRF was increased from 10 to 200 Hz. These results indicate that the SC intensity of flowing microbubbles correlates negatively with the PRF above 5 Hz when using a long PL.

Fig. 5B2 shows the temporal distribution of ultra-harmonics intensity at PRFs of 5, 10 and 200 Hz for a PL of 200 μs. The ultra-harmonics intensity was higher during the first few pulses at these PRFs, before gradually decreasing in subsequent pulses. The rate of decrease of the ultra-harmonics intensity increased with increasing PRF: at 10 Hz, the ultra-harmonic intensity remained at a relatively high value (∼0.3 × 10−10 V2 s), but at 200 Hz, it reduced to the level of the control solution after dozens of pulses. From the spectra in Fig. 5C2, the ultra-harmonic components can be seen to dominate during the first few pulses, and thereafter diminish or disappear in the following pulses for 200 Hz PRF. These results suggest that a long PL may affect the number and properties of the flowing microbubbles in the focal region, leading to a dependence of the ultra-harmonics emission on the PRF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the PRF on the intensity and temporal distribution of inertial cavitation
ce:section.ce:para: We selected a PNP of 0.42 MPa to trigger the inertial cavitation of flowing microbubbles. At different fluid velocities (16 or 24 mm/s), the intensity and temporal distribution of IC were evaluated for PRFs of 1–200 Hz for 150 short (50 μs) or long (400 μs) PLs, and the results as shown in Fig. 6A and B. For the long PL, when the PRF was varied from 1 to 5 Hz, the IC intensity at both fluid velocities was relatively constant, whereas the IC intensity gradually reduced to the minimum when PRF increased from 10 Hz to 200 Hz. Moreover, increasing the fluid velocity for the PRF below 5 Hz can’t generate significantly stronger broadband emissions, for example, approximately 14.0 × 10−8 and 15.8 × 10−8 V2·s at 2 Hz-PRF for fluid velocities of 16 and 24 mm/s, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6A. These results suggested the IC intensity was not dependent on the PRF below 5 Hz. Fig. 6B1 shows the temporal distribution of broadband emission at a fluid velocity of 16 mm/s over 150 long (400 μs) pulses at PRFs of 5, 10 and 200 Hz. For these PRFs, the broadband emissions reached a temporal peak after arrival of the first pulse, before drastically decreasing upon arrival of the second pulse, and then stabilizing at a slightly lower level for all subsequent pulses. Moreover, the decreasing rate of broadband emissions increased with increasing PRF; for example, the broadband intensity at 5, 10 and 200 Hz was about 1.2 × 10−8, 0.5 × 10−8 and ∼0 V2·s, respectively. The power spectra of acoustic signals over 150 pulses in Fig. 6C1 reveals intense broadband emissions in almost all pulses at a PRF of 5 Hz. By contrast, almost no broadband emissions were observed at 200 Hz except in the first few pulses.

The results for the short PL (50 μs) are shown in Fig. 6A. The broadband intensities were less than those at long PL (400 μs). Moreover, these intensities were constant over the PRF range of 1–5 Hz, and decreased when the PRF was increased from 10 to 200 Hz. The broadband emissions in the focal region over 150 pulses at 5, 10 and 200 Hz are shown in Fig. 6B2. The broadband intensity has a temporal peak in the first few pulses, and then sharply decreases to the relatively steady level over the next pulses. This trend is similar to that observed at long PL. The corresponding acoustic spectra over 150 pulses shown in Fig. 6C2 are consistent with that at long PL. These results suggest that the PRF — not the PL — indirectly affects the number and properties of the flowing microbubbles in the focal volume, resulting in a dependence of the IC intensity on the PRF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion
Cavitation evolution under an appropriate PRF and fluid condition
ce:section.ce:para:
Ultrasound pulsing at different PRFs and the fluid with a certain velocity complicate the microbubble population distributed in the focal volume, leading to complex cavitation properties. Assuming the velocity of the flowing microbubbles is approximately equal to the fluid velocity (v mm/s) [16], the off-time between two successive pulses determines the number and spatial distribution of newly replenished microbubbles in the focal region [40]. A minimal off-time (=
1
PRF
- P L ≈
3 v
s
) was required for the replenished microbubbles to uniformly distribute across the 3.0-mm lateral FWHM prior to the next pulse arrival, thus we defined the PRF threshold (
≈
1
minimaloff - t i m e
=
v 3
H z
) as dividing the fluid velocity by the lateral FWHM [32]. At PRFs below this threshold, the focal volume can be completely filled with flowing bubbles in the off-time, such that the same number of bubbles is uniformly distributed across the lateral FWHM distance before and during the pulse. As a result, the cavitation activity is the same in all pulses. Our results showed that cavitation evolution of flowing microbubbles is strongly dependent on the PNP at the PRF below PRF threshold. With the increasing PNP, flowing microbubbles absorb more energy, changing from linear oscillations to large-amplitude oscillation (SC), and finally collapsing (IC). The cavitation thresholds under the flow environment are approximately same with those obtained in a static environment (please see Supplementary material S1) [44], thus flow condition does not have a significant influence on cavitation thresholds. In addition, our results indicate that the cavitation thresholds of flowing bubbles reduce with the increasing PL. This is because flowing microbubbles absorb more energy from long PLs, easily undergoing large-amplitude oscillation and violent collapse, thereby leading to a reduction in cavitation thresholds. These results are also consistent with those obtained in the static environment (please see Supplementary material S2), and the previous conclusions that demonstrated a decrease in cavitation thresholds of the stationary bubbles suspension with increasing PL [7,23,30,44].

Besides the dependence of the PRF threshold on the fluid velocity, we also found the change of the fluid velocity had greater influence on the ultraharmonics intensity than on the broadband emission at the PRF below PRF threshold. Under the lower PNP for triggering SC, the bubbles could be continuously replenished into the focal region during the acoustic pulse emitted. At the same pulse duration, the faster the fluid velocity was, the more the amount of the replenished bubbles was, thus stronger ultraharmonics emission was produced in the high fluid velocity than in the low one. Differently, under the higher PNP for triggering IC, once the bubbles flowed into the rim of focal region during the acoustic pulse emitted, they immediately collapsed and didn’t maintain inside the focal region. Therefore, the fluid couldn’t carry more bubbles into the focal region during the pulse emitted, resulting in almost no difference in the broadband emission under different flow velocities. These results suggest that the flow velocity itself is an important parameter for determination of cavitation dynamics even at PRF below PRF threshold, and should be reasonably selected in application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of PRF on the PL dependence of stable cavitation properties
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies confirmed that the spatial uniformity of cavitation can be improved by exposing the bubbles at low acoustic pressure (<200 KPa) and short PL, owing to the persistence of SC at low pressures [16,41]. We found that below a PRF threshold (≈5.33 Hz at 16 mm/s fluid velocity), the PL had no influence on the temporal distribution of SC intensity. However, above this PRF threshold, the SC properties were dependent on the PL. The intensity and temporal distribution of SC were uncorrelated with the PRF at shorter PLs, whereas they were negatively correlated with the PRF at longer PLs. These conclusions are different from those obtained in static environment [7,44]. Supplementary material S3 also showed the SC intensity of bubbles in 0 mm/s velocity was positively correlated with the PL or PRF, and the ultraharmonics were produced only in the period of the first few acoustic pulses.

Below the PRF threshold, the focal volume completely fills with reperfused flowing microbubbles in the off-time. Consequently, the same number of bubbles is uniformly distributed along the lateral distance of the focal region before each pulse emitted. Thus, the SC intensity, which is spatially distributed in terms of the shape of the beam profile [40], is the same during each pulse. However, at PRFs above the PRF threshold, the off-time is insufficient for the flowing bubbles to be replenished in the focal region. It was reported in early studies that short PLs at low PNPs had little influence on the stability of the bubble shell and the bubble lifetime [7,26]. As such, some bubbles that have undergone stable oscillation after receiving a short pulse can retain their properties and remain in the focal region (moving towards the outflow part during the off-time). Thus, before arrival of the next pulse, the focal volume is filled with a mixture of the remaining bubbles (near to the outflow region) and newly replenished ones (near to the inflow region). Therefore, even above the PRF threshold, for short PLs, almost same number of bubbles is present in the focal region before each pulse, leading to approximately the same ultra-harmonics intensity in each pulse (i.e. a uniform temporal distribution). This finding is in agreement with previous studies that reported no notable difference between PRFs in the range 0.62–5 kHz (the PRF threshold is 1.7-Hz at 10 mm/s fluid velocity and 5.85-mm FWHM) when using a short PL (5 μs) [16].

Although microbubbles during low PNP-induced stable oscillation were not directly disrupted, previous reports indicated that long pulses could cause bubbles to undergo multiple expansion and contraction cycles, thus weakening the stability of the shell, resulting in the diffusion of gas through defects in the shell. This kind of bubble rupture (i.e. acoustically driven diffusion) could not release acoustic energy to the ambient environment [45]. Early studies also indicated the lifetime of bubbles at a given pressure was directly correlated with the number of the cycles [7,16]. Thus, in the present study, after receiving a long pulse, most flowing microbubbles underwent acoustically driven diffusion and then ruptured, with almost no residual bubbles (with a highly intact shell) remaining in the focal region. At PRFs above the PRF threshold, during the off-time between pulses, newly reperfused bubbles could not reach the outflow part, and were mainly distributed in the inflow part of the focal region. Therefore, the number of bubbles decreased in the focal region, leading to lower ultra-harmonics intensity with the high spatial bias around inflow region. Moreover, with increasing PRF, the number of new microbubbles replenished into the focal region gradually decreased, causing lower ultra-harmonic emissions.

The findings demonstrate that regulating the PL at the low PNP can modulate the longevity of bubble interaction with ultrasound and control the spatiotemporal distribution of cavitation activity. Increasing the PL resulted in a greater spatial bias for the inflow and reduced cavitation activity in the focal region. This may be the cause of the heterogeneous distribution of therapeutic drugs in targeted regions with long PLs [38,46]. However, short PLs, which provide the potential for extending the lifetime of the flowing bubbles, could be selected to facilitate more persistent and uniform spatial distribution of SC intensity at high PRFs, leading to a more-uniform distribution of bioeffects in the focal volume [47]. Moreover, short pulses can reduce standing waves and thermal effects in the target region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of PRF on inertial cavitation properties
ce:section.ce:para: From the supplementary materials S4, the IC intensity in static environment was noted to increase with increasing PRF. This is due to the bubbles absorbing more acoustic energy at higher PRFs. This conclusion is similar to that obtained in early study [44]. However, we observed that increasing the PRF (at either short or long PL) does not necessarily increase the IC energy of flowing bubbles. This was uncorrelated with the PRF below the PRF threshold because of complete replenishment of flowing bubbles in the focal region, but was negatively correlated with the PRF above the PRF threshold. Moreover, we observed a temporal bias in cavitation intensity distribution. As the discussed above, prior to arrival of acoustic pulses, the focal region was filled with flowing bubbles. Almost all bubbles instantaneously collapsed after receiving an acoustic pulse at PNPs above the IC threshold; thus, the peak broadband emissions could be observed in the first few pulses. The fast elimination of cavitation nuclei from the focal region necessitates a longer off-time to allow microbubble replenishment in the focal region. However, above the PRF threshold, the short off-time resulted in newly replenished bubbles being mainly present in the inflow part of the focal region, and thus not being able to reach the outflow part before arrival of the subsequent pulse. Therefore, the number of flowing bubbles decreased, resulting in a decrease in IC intensity with a spatial bias around the inflow after arrival of subsequent pulses. Previous studies using cavitation mapping also showed the IC to be spatially distributed in the inflow part when using a high PRF [40]. These studies demonstrated that the flowing conditions affected the spatiotemporal distribution of the IC activity at high PRF by influencing the number and distribution of bubbles in the focal region. Moreover, the inflow part may be overexposed because the newly replenished bubbles mostly remain in this region at PRFs above the PRF threshold. These results highlight the importance of selecting the appropriate PRF (according to the blood fluid rate in targeted volume and lateral FWHM) when IC is applied to produce a therapeutic outcome, as it may affect the dose, distribution, and therapeutic efficiency [38,46]. Several other studies have also proposed the use of PRFs below the PRF threshold to obtain spatially and temporally uniform IC activity. Additionally, this relationship between the IC properties and PRF is similar to that between the SC properties and PRF at long PLs. However, the latter is mainly due to acoustically driven diffusion resulting in a decrease in the number of bubbles in the focal region, whereas the former is due to violent bubble collapse leading to a decrease in the number of the bubbles.

On the other hand, from the point of the bubble stability, under the PRFs below PRF threshold, although the amount of bubbles in the focal region before the acoustic pulse emitted was same, the lifetime of these bubbles may be different due to non-uniform size distribution (large bubbles may have relatively long lifetime) [31], resulting in the change of the number of the bubbles during the acoustic pulse emitted. Moreover, when the PNP was above IC threshold, most of bubbles in a few acoustic cycles may disintegrate into daughter bubbles, leading to the change of the bubbles number [48,49]. The temporal fluctuation in the number of the bubbles during pulse emitted also affected the broadband emissions, thereby probably influencing the IC dynamics in the focal region [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summative assess
ce:section.ce:para: This study did not perform a wide range of vasculature sizes and fluid velocities, because the size of the vasculature (especially in small-size vessels like the capillaries) can constrain the maximum expansion of the oscillating bubbles, and the fluid velocity may change the shear stress of the liquid environment, thereby influencing the cavitation activity. A substantially more-sophisticated phantom design is required to enhance physiologically relevant condition in future studies. In addition, although our theoretical analysis of the spatial distribution of cavitation activity in the focal region is consistent with previous studies, it is difficult to determine the spatial distribution of cavitation activity in the focal region using our experimental system. More importantly, a flow speed in the vasculature is not constant, slower beside the vasculature wall and faster at the center of the vasculature. This effect may cause non-uniform distribution of bubble number in the radial direction, resulting in non-uniform distribution of cavitation activity in this direction. Thus, future studies need to employ some advanced measurement methods with high spatiotemporal resolution, such as passive cavitation mapping, to evaluate the spatial distribution of cavitation in the radial and flowing directions of the focal volume. In addition, the bioeffects induced under the optimal conditions identified in this study need to be investigated in future experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: This investigation determined that the number and properties of the replenished bubbles into the focal volume were influenced by the fluid velocity and a pulsed acoustic field, leading to the change of cavitation properties. We found that the transition between cavitation types depended on PNP, and the cavitation thresholds weakly depended on PL. We also identified a PRF threshold to ensure that the focal region is filled with flowing bubbles in the off-time between two successive pulses. Below this PRF threshold, the PL had no influence on the temporal distribution of SC intensity. However, above this PRF threshold, the PL affected the SC properties owing to acoustically driven diffusion. Moreover, below the PRF threshold, IC properties were independent of the PRF. But the IC intensity negatively correlated with the PRF above the PRF threshold. These results could guide the design of new acoustic pulses for future applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81471667, 81471666), Program of Medicine and Engineering Cross Fund of Shanghai Jiao Tong University (YG2015ZD09, ZH2018QNA21, YG2015QN36) and Rising Star Fund of Shanghai Jiao Tong University (16 X100080028)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2018.12.031.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
